data.title: FAS: Significant Risks Warrant A Sell Rating
data.description:
I rate the Direxion Daily Financial Bull 3X Shares ETF (NYSEARCA:FAS) a Sell due to six major risks I see for financial stocks. Given the +3x leverage of FAS, this is a particularly risky time for this ETF. There will be good times to buy this ETF in the future, but given the risks I see for the economy, inflation, interest rates, bonds, housing and commercial real estate, now is not the time.

ETF Overview

The Direxion Daily Financial Bull 3X Shares ETF (FAS) targets +3x daily long leveraged returns of the Russell 1000 Financial Services Index, so it is a very highly leveraged bet on a continued rise in financial stocks.

FAS was launched by Rafferty Asset Management in November 2008, at the peak of the Global Financial Crisis. It is a sizable fund with $2.77 billion in assets. which makes it much larger than the ProShares Ultra Financials ETF (UYG) at $859 million. UYG is a similar fund that targets +2x daily long leveraged returns of the S&P Financial Select Sector Index. FAS liquidity is good with average daily dollar volume of $90 million. That is 90x more than UYG. The expense ratio of 0.89% is below the UYG expense ratio of 0.94%. The dividend yield of 0.93% is below the 1.04% yield of UYG and the 1.40% yield of the Financial Select Sector SPDR Fund ETF.

Investors interested in FAS should understand that the fund targets +3x returns on a daily basis. Due to the compounding of daily returns, the returns for holding FAS for periods longer than a day can be very different from triple the returns of the Russell 1000 Financial Services Index. If the index is in a relatively steady trend during the holding period, the return of FAS will often be more than triple the Index return, which is a nice bonus. But for highly volatile trading periods, the return on FAS can be much less than triple the Index return, which is a major risk of this fund. For example, if the Index rises 10% one day, falls 10% the next day, rises 10% the following day and then falls 10% the next day, the Index return over those four days will be about negative 2%, But the FAS will be about negative 17%!

That is why I caution against buying any +3x leveraged fund unless you are highly confident in a strongly bullish outlook for the underlying index.

Since I believe there are significant downside risks for the index, I rate FAS a Sell.

Investment Thesis

The ideal environment for financial stocks, particularly for a +3x levered long financial stock ETF, is the following:

  • bull market in stocks, bonds, real estate and other assets
  • steadily growing economy
  • moderate or falling interest rates
  • moderate or slowing inflation

Unfortunately, I think the current outlook is not supportive of such an ideal environment, which is what is needed to justify investing in a +3x levered long financials ETF like FAS.

Here are the top six risks I see facing financial stocks

1. Recession and Credit Risk

In 2022, the Fed embarked on the most aggressive rate hiking cycle in over 40 years to try to fight the inflation they created when they increased the money supply by 40% in response to the covid lockdowns. This resulted in the longest yield curve inversion in US history and the deepest money supply decline since the Great Depression. The unemployment rate has risen by 0.9% over the past two years, which is more than the 0.5% rise that typically coincides with a recession. Also, The Conference Board’s Leading Economic index has been declining for nearly four years and is currently flashing a recession warning. Thus, I believe recession risk is currently high.

A recession would weaken loan demand and lead to higher credit losses, defaults and delinquencies, which would hurt commercial banks. Investment banks and insurance companies would be hurt by the falling stock prices and asset values that typically accompany recessions. Wealth managers and brokers would be hit particularly hard. And a bear market in stocks would be devastating for a +3x levered long fund like FAS.

2. Stubbornly High And Potentially Rising Inflation

Despite their aggressive rate hikes, the Fed has consistently failed to hit their 2% inflation target for many years. Inflation has been accelerating modestly lately on many measures and Trump’s tariffs (i.e., taxes on imports) may lead to even more inflation acceleration going forward. This would likely be negative for interest rates and stock and bond prices, which would be bearish for financial stocks and their earnings.

3. Rising Interest Rates and Net Interest Margin Compression

Banks typically borrow short-term and lend long-term. They pay interest on short-term liabilities like deposits and earn interest on long-term loans and bonds. The spread between what they earn on long-term lending and what they pay on short-term borrowing is their net interest margin. That is what drives the profitability of banks. If inflation forces the Fed to reverse their recent course and raise short-term rates, that could negatively impact bank earnings , as well as likely lead to significant weakness in stocks, particularly financial stocks.

4. Bond Portfolio Losses

The rapid rise in long-term interest rates due to the high inflation the Fed created resulted in substantial unrealized losses on the bond portfolios of banks, particularly on Treasury bonds and mortgage-backed securities. While banks are not required to mark these losses to market, there is still economic risk if they need to sell those securities to meet liquidity needs. US. banks currently have $395 billion in unrealized losses on investment securities, as shown here.

5. Housing Market Weakness And Mortgage Risk

The combination of record high home prices and higher mortgage rates have driven housing affordability to record lows. This has resulted in a major drop in mortgage origination volumes. If home prices should fall to back to earth relative to incomes and rent, banks could face higher defaults on mortgages. The current housing bubble is even bigger than the one that triggered the Great Recession of 2008-2009, as shown here.

6. Commercial Real Estate Weakness

The covid pandemic caused a major drop in office occupancy rates. Higher interest rates and falling property values are raising refinancing risks. Many banks have significant exposure to commercial real estate loans. The Office CMBS Delinquency Rate is now at an all-time high of 11.7%. That is even higher than the Great Recession high of 10.7%, as shown here.

These are formidable risks facing financial stocks. Any one of these could spell serious trouble for financial stocks. A combination of two or more could lead to significant downside in FAS.

ETF Holdings

FAS typically invests in financial instruments such as swap and futures contracts, stocks held by the Index and ETFs that track the Index in order to target +3x daily leveraged exposure to the Index.

The table below from Seeking Alpha shows the current top 10 holdings of FAS, which includes leading large cap US financial services companies, although I view Berkshire Hathaway as more of a conglomerate.

This table from the fund’s website shows the stock and sector weightings of the Index.

Valuation Analysis

Financial stocks trade around 16x EPS and 2.2x book value for 11% estimated long-term EPS growth. That compares favorably to the S&P 500, which trades at 23x EPS and 4.5x book value for 10% long-term growth.

But given that US stocks are trading near all time high valuation levels — even higher than at the 1929 and 2000 peaks — we believe downside risk is high for the S&P 500 in the event of a bear market caused by a recession, higher inflation, higher interest rates or some combination of those. This chart from economist and fund manager John Hussman shows the best stock market valuation metric that correlates with future 10-12 year returns is near the all-time highest levels of the past century.

Technical Analysis

The chart below show FAS monthly price chart along with its 12-month moving average over the past 15 years (top clip). It has been flat for nearly a year and is only 2% above its critical 12-month moving average.

The second clip shows its price performance relative to the S&P 500. FAS has been underperforming the S&P 500 for nearly a year, which is a concerning sign. I have highlighted with blue boxes periods where FAS significantly underperformed the S&P 500. These were periods when the entire market was down, such as the 2020 covid crash and the 2022 bear market. It makes sense that a +3x leveraged ETF would underperform significantly in a down market. That is why investing in this fund requires a bullish outlook on both financial stocks and the overall stock market.

The third clip shows long-term momentum, based on the Percentage Price Oscillator (PPO), has recently turned negative, with the black line falling below the red line. I have highlighted with blue boxes prior periods when the PPO turned down, all of which preceded significant declines in FAS.

Risks

The primary risks to our bearish outlook for FAS would be an improvement in the economic environment. An improving economic outlook, possibly driven by a continuation of the AI investment boom and a reduction of Trump’s tariffs, along with falling inflation and interest rates, could help sustain the current high valuations of stocks and housing and lead to rising bond prices. I would welcome this “nirvana” for financial stocks, but I do not see that happening anytime soon.

Conclusion

I think there are major headwinds facing US financial stocks. These include recession risk, inflation risk, interest rate risk, bond loss risk, housing market risk and commercial real estate risks. Each one of these risks could pressure the earnings of financial stocks and lead to negative investor sentiment and lower valuations over the coming year or two.

Now is not the time to buy FAS, so I rate it a Sell.

data.title: UTES: Increasing Power Demands Provide Continued Strong Tailwinds
data.description:
In early April, shortly after the market nosedive that occurred after tariff “Liberation Day,” I wrote my first analysis of the Virtus Reaves Utilities ETF (NYSEARCA:UTES), calling the fund a buy. The actively managed fund had a strong track record of past performance that included a gain in net asset value of over 45% in 2024. Since my article was published, UTES has gained over 48% when dividends are included, a remarkable six-month run for a portfolio of stocks that includes a number of regulated utility companies. UTES is not your father’s stodgy utility fund, however. It also includes independent power producers and energy traders who are benefiting from the projections for power demands caused by data centers that will support artificial intelligence.

With such a sharp price increase in a relatively short period of time, I felt it was necessary to revisit my previous analysis to see if this is still a fund that is deserving of new investments, or if perhaps profit-taking is in order. While it is unreasonable to expect the same returns in the next six months that were seen since April of this year, I think there are still several reasons that UTES could be a solid addition to a growth portfolio that is looking for diversification away from traditional market index funds. Increased demand for power in the US is not going away any time soon, and the stocks included in this fund represent some of the companies that stand to benefit the most from these trends.

ETF Overview And Past Performance

The Virtus Reaves Utilities ETF is actively managed by Virtus Investment Partners with Reaves Asset Management as a subadvisor. The fund is highly concentrated in a relatively small number of stocks. As of October 15, 2025, the fund reported only 18 equity holdings, one money market holding, and one cash position. According to the fund’s fact sheet, UTES has the goal of maximizing total return through both capital appreciation and income from stocks in the utility sector, but its performance leans heavily on the side of growth and capital appreciation.

The fund managers tout three unique features for UTES. The first is a stronger risk/return model that, when compared to passively managed utility funds like the Utility Select Sector SPDR Fund (XLU) or the Vanguard Utilities ETF (VPU). The second is sector expertise provided by Reaves Asset Management through a long-term strategy-focused approach. The final strength of the fund, according to Virtus, is active management that uses both qualitative and quantitative analysis to drive “bottom-up security selection.”

Whether looking at the returns from the last six months or looking at the returns from the last decade, it is hard to argue with the results from the fund. UTES has outperformed the S&P 500 Utilities Index by over 4% annually since 2015. In the last 12 months, it has rewarded investors with a gain of over 32%, while the index has returned just over 11%. The fund has a five-star rating from Morningstar, as the financial services provider has placed the fund in the top 1% of funds in its category in both 2024 and 2025.

Below is a list of the top 10 stock holdings as of October 15, 2025. When I last wrote about the fund in April, almost a third of the entire portfolio was allocated to its top four holdings. Now, there is just over a third of the fund in its top three. However, UTES is slightly less top-heavy than it was at the end of last year, when its top four stocks made up just over 48% of its portfolio. Comparing this list to the fund’s allocations from April, NiSource (NI), Alliant Energy (LNT), and Public Service Enterprise Group (PEG) are all new additions to the top 10. Missing from the previous rankings are Sempra (SRE), Atmos Energy (ATO), and PPL Corporation (PPL).

There is little doubt that the top three stocks in the UTES portfolio have all been significant drivers of the fund’s performance this year. After recording a triple-digit gain in 2024, Vistra (VST) has gained another 52.9% year-to-date. Talen Energy (TLN) has gained 106.4% since January 1 of this year. Constellation Energy (CEG), another member of the triple-digit gainer club from 2024, is up 80.6% in 2025.

The management fee charged for UTES ownership is 0.49%. This is certainly higher than many of its passively managed peers like XLU or VPU that charge under 0.10%. However, it is hard to quibble about an additional 40 basis points in fees when the fund is outperforming at such a consistently high rate.

ETF Analysis

A recent report from S&P Global stated that demand for power from data centers is expected to grow by over 20% this year. The long-range projections are even more substantial, as power demand may come close to tripling by 2030. Crypto mining and artificial intelligence are two of the larger trends that are fueling this boom. However, previous reports have noted that electricity needs from residential sources are also rising, with a double-digit percentage increase expected by 2030.

Utilities are also able to charge more for electricity than they have in the past, as regulators have been quite amenable to recent rate increase requests. Rate increases for 2025 have been described as “unprecedented” because they have surpassed the already strong year for rising rates in 2024. While there are notable affordability concerns for consumers, there appear to be no signs that this trend is slowing down.

I admit that there are some valuation concerns to be had with many of the top holdings in this fund. The recent string of triple-digit returns mentioned earlier cannot happen on a regular basis. Many of the top holdings in UTES are trading at profit multiples that would be considered elevated for most traditional growth stocks but are especially unusual for power producers and utilities.

However, a deeper dive shows that some of the valuations may not be that out of line. Consider the fund’s top three holdings – Vistra, Talen Energy, or Constellation Energy. Each of them trades at a PE multiple well above its sector median. Talen even trades at a forward PE higher than that of NVIDIA (NVDA).

Forward PE Ratio

Forward PEG Ratio

VST

29.28

1.29

TLN

76.10

1.80

CEG

41.35

2.71

When earnings growth is considered, however, the numbers do not look as preposterous. As a matter of fact, VST and TLN get grades of “A” and “A-” respectively from SA Quant Rating for their PEG ratios when compared to their sector medians. Even CEG has a PEG ratio that is only three basis points above its sector median. There is no denying that these stocks and many of the others that are owned by UTES are at a higher risk level than most of the stocks one would expect to find in a utility fund, but the higher risk has led to greater gains for these stocks in the past, and the market seems to be on a continued path to rewarding these companies in the future as the demand for their services continues to remain elevated.

Risks To Consider

Other utility ETFs like XLU and VPU offer investors the added protection of a relatively high dividend yield. Should those funds lose value or stagnate over a long period of time, they offer payout rates of 2.5% that can provide some income to soften the blow of capital losses. UTES does not have the same benefit. Its current yield of 1.15% is even lower than that currently offered by the iShares Core S&P 500 ETF (IVV). Income investors who may normally be attracted to the more traditional regular utilities held by XLU and VPU will not have the same interest in UTES, and growth investors who have bought into this fund because of its strong share price performance may quickly turn on its components when previous trends are reversed.

UTES is actively managed, and there are no guarantees that the fund managers can match their previous performance or outperform the passively managed utility index funds in the future. UTES has a Sharpe Ratio of 1.14, showing that it has produced strong risk-adjusted returns in the past. However, there are a number of macroeconomic factors that could work against the stocks held in its portfolio in the future, including higher interest rates, decreased demand for power, or reduced spending on data centers. Morningstar gives the fund a rating of high risk, noting that it often captures 100% of the downside in the broader market, unlike other utility funds that capture only about 92%.

When I last wrote about this fund, I mentioned the concentration of its portfolio being an important risk to consider. At that time, there were 22 stocks held by UTES. Now that number stands at just 18, with even greater concentration in its top three holdings. Should the performance of Vistra, Talen Energy, or Constellation Energy head south in the months ahead, it would put significant pressure on the overall performance of the fund.

The Bottom Line

When I last wrote about UTES, the fund was down 6% for the year. The situation is quite different now, with the sizeable gains likely tempting investors toward profit-taking. Admittedly, the fund will likely see added volatility in the months ahead as the threat of an AI "bubble" appears to put the fund's portfolio at even higher risk. Dollar cost averaging certainly could be one way to mitigate some of the hazards associated with a new investment in the fund, but the same market tailwinds that existed in April still appear to be just as strong today. I still consider UTES to be a buy heading into the start of the new year.

data.title: Why I Monitor Junk Bonds And What I See Now
data.description:
Junk bond yield is my depth indicator

These days, my inbox is crowded by alarms about the equity market with titles like “the top 5 signs that the SP500 is peaked” or “the top 2 killers for this bull market”. After reading a few of them, a common theme for these signs and killers begin to emerge and they include factors (or their variations) like the Shiller P/E Ratio (CAPE, which is admittedly elevated by historical norm), the Buffett Indicator (which is admittedly also highly stretched by historical norm), the S&P 500 (SP500) P/E (which indeed exceeds modern averages by a good margin), the thin or even negative equity risk premium as measured by SP500 expected return (e.g., Earnings Yield or dividend yields) relative to risk-free rates (e.g., 10-Year U.S. Treasury Yield (US10Y), and also the excessive dependence of the overall market on a few Mega-Cap stocks (such as the mag 7). We’ve written recently to express our view on the above issues and offered our counter arguments (a most recent example is in our article titled “S&P 500: A Bear Thesis Always Sounds More Exciting”).

However, we felt our earlier counterarguments focused more on the limitations of the above indicators without offering an alternative indicator, which is what this article aims to accomplish. The remainder sections of this article will explain why we monitor junk bond yield closely and what we are seeing now. In particular, we use the yield of junk bonds to gauge the depth of a market rally and corrections. From experiences, we observed it to be a critical depth indicator for the S&P 500 index because it reflects the underlying investor risk appetite more sensitively than other assets such as treasury bonds or investment grade bonds. Many (probably most) investors hold considerable positions of treasury and investment grade bonds regardless of market conditions. But due to their sub-par credit rating, junk bonds are highly sensitive to economic forecasts and potential corporate default risk. Therefore, the pricing of these bonds provides an early warning signal often preceding moves in the equity market and/or the overall bond market. When even junk bonds are highly priced, it is a telling sign in our experience that investors’ optimism has both gone depth and wide.

And this is what we are seeing now. To wit, the chart below shows the yield from the Vanguard High-Yield Corporate Fund Investor Shares (VWEHX) since 1980. This is the oldest junk bond mutual fund to our knowledge with an inception date in 1978. It took 1~2 years for its yield to stabilize, so the chart plots data starting 1980. As seen, the current yield of 6.14% is not only far below the historical average of 7.8%, but is also among the lowest levels in the past 4 decades or so. We will next elaborate on what this means for the equity market.

Review of historical data

Let me first digress a minute to explain why I like to use VWEHX as proxy for “junk bond,” which is more a less a vague concept. The definition I follow is that any bond rated below the Investment Grade Threshold is classified as a junk bond. This threshold means ratings of BB+ and lower by S&P or Fitch standard or Ba1 and lower by Moody's standard. According to this definition, the VWEHX fund's credit exposure is heavily concentrated in below-investment-grade debt as you can see from the following screenshot. The fund’s largest single allocation is to BB-rated bonds, which account for a substantial 49.67%. Following this, the B-rated category comprises another significant portion at 33.82%. The portfolio also maintains a 4.71% exposure to the highest-risk segment, specifically bonds rated CCC or Lower. As such, the fund collectively places around 88% of its assets the junk bond market following the definition I use here.

As a second reason, VWEHX’s long history provides a wealth of data for us to evaluate its long-term effectiveness as shown immediately next.

The chart below illustrates the relationship between the SPDR® S&P 500 ETF Trust (NYSEARCA:SPY) price movement (top panel) and the VWEHX yield spread relative to 10-year treasury rates (the bottom panel). Even visual examination would reveal a quite strong inverse correlation. The arrows we added highlight this correlation at key market junctures such as the dot-com bubble, the 2008 financial crisis, the 2010 Europe bank crisis, and also the more recent COVID 19 episode. As seen, in all cases, major spikes in the high-yield spread reliably signaled the depth of equity market corrections as shown by the red arrows. For instance, the Great Financial Crisis saw the spread surge to its highest recorded point of 8.81, directly coinciding with the SPY’s deepest decline. Conversely, when the SPY price is near its peak, the spread is compressed, reflecting investors’ enthusiasm even toward assets with sub-par assets as aforementioned. And in all the key market junctures, a sizable correction occurred shortly, as highlighted by the green arrows.

Under the current market conditions, the spread between VWEHX and 10-year treasure rate hovers around 2.132% as seen. It is below historical average of 3.233% by a good margin and also quite tight by historical standard. In other words, I consider the current situation one of the “green-arrow cases” where investors’ risks appetite has extended wide and deep to include assets with sub-par credit ratings, which in turn drive down their yield and lead to the narrower-than-average yield spread we are seeing now. Given the spread’s role as a barometer of depth and risk sentiment in the past, this is what concerns me more than the other indicators mentioned earlier (SP500 CAPE ratio, P/E ratio, Buffett index, heavy tilt towards the tech mega-caps, etc.). Most companies in the SP500 are financially strong with investment credit ratings, and the tech mega-caps indeed enjoy superb profitability. Of course, paying a premium for quality assets entails risks too. But past experiences have taught me the it's far more risky to pay a premium on assets with sub-par financial strengths.

Other risks and final thoughts

The picture is equally concerning if we broaden the scope and also time horizontal as shown in the next chart. This chart tracks the Moody's Seasoned Baa Corporate Bond Yield relative to the Yield on the $10-Year Treasury Constant Maturity (BAA10Y) for an even longer period of time. This spread, a key measure of credit risk for investment-grade debt, currently sits at 1.71% as of this writing, which is again among the thinnest levels throughout this nearly 40-year period. Again, you can see that this spread has historically spiked during U.S. recessionary periods (indicated by the gray-shaded areas) and also narrowed substantially during market peaks before the recessions.

On the positive side, according to the latest dot-plot announced by the Federal Open Market Committee (FOMC, see the chart below), the median projection shows a clear path for a series of interest rate cuts in the next 2~3 years. More specifically, policymakers have indicated a median expectation for the federal funds rate to fall to a range of 3.50% to 3.75% by the end of 2026. Assuming policymakers do follow this dot-plot, investors can expect a total rate cuts around 50 basis-points in the next 1~2 years. These cuts should help to reduce the tension between junk bond yield and treasury rates yield. Assuming everything else to be equal and also assuming long-term rates change in tandem with short term rates (which are purely for illustrative purposes), these cuts could potentially widen the yield spread between VWEHX and 10-year treasury rats to 2.63% (vs. the current 2.13%), which would be still below the long-term average of 3.23%.

To close, let me reiterate a key lesson we’ve learned in our investing journey: we (and I bet you also) cannot time the market. We admit there are a bunch of parameters that suggest a “market peak,” but we also learned that A) these indicators all have limitations and could become even outdated, and B) the risk of making drastic allocation changes based on these indicators far outweighs the risks of staying invested even at the so-called “market peaks.” As an additional/alternative indicator, we monitor junk bond yields. The picture we see is certainly concerning and worth closely monitoring, but it is not alarming to trigger a dramatic asset reallocation in our model. As such, in our portfolios, we are adjusting our equity exposure dynamics and gradually based on a discipline model (which evaluates the relative valuation of equity and bonds following ideas similar to those illustrated in this article). We suggest you follow a disciplined approach, too.

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Magnite Stock: Buy The Dip For Above Average Growth
data.description:
Magnite (NASDAQ:MGNI) looks like a solid combination of strong long-term above-average growth with a low valuation. The company has multiple positive growth factors that can drive the stock for above-average long-term gains. The stock's recent pullback presents a good buy-the-dip opportunity for long-term investors.

Magnite's Company Background

Magnite operates as a $2.5 billion market cap New York City-based sell-side advertising company. The company's platform helps sellers of digital advertising inventory or publishers that operate CTV [connected TV] channels, websites, applications, and other digital media. Magnite helps advertising sellers to manage and monetize their inventory. MGNI helps advertising buyers (ad agencies/demand side platforms, etc.) to buy digital advertising inventory. The company also brings sellers and buyers together through its independent marketplace. Magnite uses sales teams to market its solutions.

In a nutshell, Magnite's solutions automate the buying and selling of digital advertising inventory. The company stated that they are the largest independent omni-channel sell-side advertising platform in the world. MGNI also stated that they are the largest independent programmatic CTV marketplace, making it easier for buyers to reach CTV audiences at scale from industry-leading broadcasters, streaming content providers, platforms, and device manufacturers. Magnite's platform processes trillions of ad requests per month.

Magnite obtains its revenue from a percentage of the ad spend on its platform. MGNI also earns revenue from certain clients, transaction types, and services through a fixed CPM for each impression sold. Magnite earns revenue for ad campaigns that are transacted through insertion orders on the full amount of ad spend that runs through the company's platform.

Magnite's Growth Catalysts

Magnite has a number of factors that are driving long-term growth. The company is experiencing strong growth for CTV and for DV+, the company's platform. CTV revenue increased 15% yoy (excluding political) while DV+ increased 8% in Q2 2025.

The growth from CTV was driven by positive SMB (small/medium sized business) trends, new & expanding partnerships, programmatic growth in live sports, and growth in agency marketplaces. Magnite obtained the most significant growth impact from Netflix (NFLX), Roku (ROKU), Warner Brothers Discovery (WBD), Paramount (PSKY), and LG in Q2.

Magnite's DV+ growth was driven by new product functionality and early contributions from recently announced partners. MGNI is experiencing market share gains for DV+ from some of the largest DSPs. Looking ahead, Magnite sees growth from additional publisher launches that are expected to begin this year, including T-Mobile, Spotify, and Redfin.

Looking forward, Magnite sees midsized direct-to-consumer brands as the most compelling future source of CTV growth. The company is already seeing momentum for that right now. One of the factors driving this is that the technology to run programmatically was already implemented by the largest publishers, leaving plenty of growth from the smaller sized publishers. Another factor is that inventory has scaled and CPMs have normalized to drive a higher return on ad spending. The use of AI is also a driver as it lowered production costs, making CTV a desired high-performing channel for digital-first advertisers.

Key Partnerships

Back in July 2025, Magnite established a strategic partnership with dentsu to accelerate innovation and performance across the media supply chain. As part of the partnership, dentsu is using Magnite's built-for-video tools and technology to power dentsu Total TV. Dentsu also plans to lead the 'Algorithmic Era' of advertising. The key factors of this era are interoperability, automation, and outcome driven planning.

Dentsu is using Magnite's SpringServe video platform for more efficient data-rich connections to inventory, enabling the company to curate premium media experiences with better transparency, precision, and scale. This is being done in EMEA markets such as the U.K. and Spain. No dollar value was given for this deal, but it demonstrates an example of the ongoing partnerships that Magnite fosters to drive higher returns for their clients.

Magnite also established a deal with FanDuel Sports Network, which produces over 3,000 live sporting events annually. FanDuel achieved a 25% yoy increase in total impressions served by using Magnite's SpringServe video platform. FanDuel sees Magnite's solutions as a key way to increase monetization in an era where more people are streaming live sports. MGNI sees live sports as a good growth opportunity as the leading players are choosing Magnite for the company's effective tech solutions.

MGNI is experiencing success in the commerce media space. The company's partner list in this space includes Kinective Media by United Airlines (UAL), RE/MAX (RMAX), PayPal (PYPL), and Western Union (WU). Magnite expects commerce media to be a another ongoing growth avenue.

Magnite has an ongoing partnership with Amazon (AMZN) as a publisher and DSP. Magnite is only one of three platforms that were approved for Amazon DSP spend. This resulted in significant growth for MGNI. Magnite expects to benefit from the trend of DSPs which are consolidating their spend to a handful of platforms that can provide access to all types of programmatic media in a safe and transparent environment. MGNI appears confident that they are one of these chosen platforms.

Another growth area is the curator business. Magnite's Curator Marketplace enables clients to operate and scale their businesses on the platform across all inventory types and screens. MGNI added nearly 50 curators since the start of Q2 2025.

AI as a Growth Driver

Magnite is developing AI and embedding it into its solutions. The company expanded its neural net and machine learning systems to shape the outbound connections to its CTV buyers. Magnite's shaping sends the most relevant available supply to bandwidth-constrained DSPs, allowing them to more efficiently discover inventory and increase their spend on the company's platform.

MGNI also has an AI-powered audience discovery feature within the Curator Marketplace tool that incorporates 3rd party data and the company's proprietary segments. This makes it easier for users to identify high-value audiences for clients' campaign objectives.

Magnite is using large language models [LLMs] using AI to automatically categorize CTV inventory into contextual segments. This makes it more addressable and drives increased campaign reach and monetization as compared to other standard methods. Magnite plans to continue to incorporate AI into its solutions including AI agents and automated optimization going forward to improve outcomes for clients.

Favorable Court Ruling Involving Google

In September 2025, the U.S. Department of Justice won a case against Google (GOOG) (GOOGL). The ruling prohibits Google from entering or maintaining exclusive contracts relating to the distribution of Chrome, Google Search, Google Assistant, and the Gemini app. Google was ordered to make certain search index and user interaction data available to rivals and potential rivals. Google was also ordered to offer search and search text ads syndication services to enable rivals and potential rivals to compete.

Magnite expects this ruling to change the landscape of open internet and drive significant upside for the company's DV+ solution. MGNI also believes that the ruling will result in a more fair and transparent process that yields higher returns for advertisers and publishers. A more level playing field as a result of this decision should result in Magnite being better able to monetize publishers' inventory.

Valuation

Since Magnite has multiple years of strong expected above-average earnings growth, I like the PEG ratio as the best valuation metric for the stock. Magnite is trading with a PEG of 0.64. This is below the sector median PEG of 1.49. Magnite's PEG is based on its 3 to 5 year expected annual earnings growth rate of 30%. I typically find that the companies that I cover tend to perform well when the PEG ratio is below two. So, below one is an even better bargain.

Magnite's strong expected EPS growth rate can be driven by the company's strong expected annual revenue growth of 10% to 15% over the same period. Then, margin growth could drive earnings to hit the high expected growth rates. MGNI increased its gross margin from 61.6% in Q2 2024 to 62.5% in Q2 2025. The operating income margin increased from 5.9% to 12.7% while the net income margin increased from a negative (0.66%) to a positive 6.43% over the same time.

Magnite's low valuation leaves plenty of room for stock appreciation as the company continues to grow at an above-average pace.

Technical Perspective

We can see in Magnite's daily chart above that the stock recently bounced higher from an oversold condition. The oversold condition occurred when the stock dropped below the 30 level on the RSI in the middle of the chart. The RSI is now back above the oversold level and looks like it just crossed above its yellow moving average line which is positive.

The MACD did not yet confirm a change in trend since the blue line did not yet cross above the red signal line. However, the histogram bars have shortened in length, indicating that the negative momentum has waned. It is possible that the blue MACD line crosses above the red signal line within a few days if the recent recovery from the oversold condition continues.

The next likely catalyst for Magnite is the Q3 earnings report which is scheduled for November 5, 2025. Analysts are expecting revenue of $163.3 million and normalized EPS of $0.20 for Q3. Positive results and upbeat forward guidance from Magnite would likely be a catalyst to keep a rally going.

Risks for Magnite

Magnite depends on the health of the advertising business. The company highly depends on the health of the CTV market. An overall reduction of ad spending or a downturn in the CTV market could have a significant negative impact on Magnite's revenue and earnings.

Magnite competes with larger companies that have more financial and other resources at their disposal. This makes for an intense competitive market in the advertising space. It is possible that one or more of these competitors develops solutions that are more attractive than Magnite's. This could lead to a loss of market share or cause MGNI to struggle to grow market share.

Magnite's Long-Term Outlook

Magnite's multiple growth catalysts are likely to help drive the company's revenue and earnings for above average stock gains over multiple years. MGNI has effective solutions for its clients. This can help attract new clients for improved advertising outcomes.

Magnite's low valuation leaves room for the stock to grow at a strong above-average pace, driven by the company's above-average revenue and earnings growth. As a result, I expect Magnite to outperform the broader market over the long term.

data.title: BSR REIT: Attractively Valued Ahead Of Q3 2025 Earnings
data.description:
Introduction

So far in 2025, BSR Real Estate Investment Trust (OTCPK:BSRTF) (TSX:HOM.U:CA) has marginally underperformed the Vanguard Real Estate Index Fund ETF (VNQ), delivering a total return close to 0%, roughly 4% lower than the broad U.S. REIT ETF.

This comes as the company has been very active in selling and acquiring assets, as discussed in my prior coverage in June 2025. As it turns out, the title I chose, "Elevated Portfolio Activity Clouds Outlook", turned out to be spot on, as BSR opted to suspend its financial guidance, citing significant disposition and acquisition activity.

Looking ahead to Q3 2025 earnings, while BSR did acquire an additional property during the quarter for $87.5 million, I reckon that the company will have a better grasp on run-rate financials, allowing BSR to reinstate its guidance. As such, I confirm my buy rating on BSR, justified by:

  • An attractive 15.5x AFFO multiple.
  • A market-implied cap rate of 7.4%, well-above the average for residential peers.
  • A ~30% discount to net asset value.

In this article, I will expand on these points and discuss risks related to the investment case.

Company Overview

You can access all BSR results on its website here. BSR is a residential REIT focused on the Texas Triangle, which accounts for 89% of its net operating income. The remaining assets are located in Oklahoma and Arkansas:

From the snippet above, we observe that the portfolio has grown to 7,170 apartment units following the latest acquisitions. We further note that while net asset value per unit is little changed year-to-date ($16.74/unit at the end of H1 2025), the company has been selling higher quality stabilized properties (occupancy of 95.7%) and reinvesting the proceeds in opportunistic acquisitions that are yet to reach full operating momentum (occupancy of 81.2%):

I estimate that the REIT's enterprise value amounts to $1.19 billion, of which 39% is funded with common equity, leaving 61% for net debt.

Q3 2025 Earnings Preview

The company is set to report Q3 2025 earnings on November 5, 2025. I think the key driving factors going into earnings will be:

  • The full impact of a $141 million acquisition closed on May 15, 2025 (which only contributed to Q2 2025 earnings for 45 days instead of the typical 90-day quarterly earnings period).
  • The full impact of a $193 million disposition closed on April 30, 2025, (which only contributed to Q2 2025 earnings for 60 days)
  • The partial impact of the Q3 2025 acquisition for $87.5 million, which will contribute to earnings for roughly 47 days.
  • The associated impact of higher leverage used to fund these net acquisitions.

BSR values its properties at a capitalization rate of 5.2%. Assuming acquisitions were closed at similar rates (which is a conservative assumption given that occupancy was below the average for the portfolio) and financed at 6% (the company's marginal cost of floating-rate debt), we see that the effect on AFFO should be negative.

My calculations show that the effect on AFFO should be under half a cent per unit. Hence my outlook for Q3 2025 AFFO is $0.19/share, in line with the one reported in Q2 2025. I see risks as tilted to the upside in the event that BSR was able to purchase properties at a cap rate above the 5.2% average for the whole portfolio.

BSR Valuation

The shares currently trade at a 30% discount to the H1 2025 net asset value of $16.74/share, indicating that the market-implied cap rate is higher than the 5.2% used to value the portfolio.

Assuming circa 52% leverage at the stated IFRS property value of $1.4 billion, I estimate that the market-implied cap rate is 7.4%, which is very attractive for a residential REIT.

Of course the million-dollar question is how much rents will grow from here. In Q2 2025, same community rental rates were 0.7% lower than prior leases, driven by a 3.7% decline in new leases, which was partially offset by a 1.7% increase in renewals.

As such, while the current valuation in terms of a 7.4% market-implied cap rate is highly attractive, the outlook for rent growth is quite muted over the near-term. The good news is that same-community rents were 1.1% higher in July 2025, indicating some strength early in Q3 2025.

All in all, I see BSR's rents growing in line or marginally below inflation over the next few years, which will put pressure on operating margins (as costs should rise largely in line with inflation). Even so, I see the 7.4% market-implied cap rate as quite attractive, especially in light of the outlook for lower risk-free interest rates (which will increase the demand for safe residential property).

To conclude, while financial visibility has clearly been clouded by recent portfolio transactions, I expect BSR to deliver AFFO in line with the one in Q2 2025. This puts the company's AFFO multiple at 15.5x, quite attractive for a residential REIT.

The company is also cheap on an enterprise-level basis, with a 7.4% market-implied cap rate well above the average for residential REITs, notwithstanding near-term margin and revenue headwinds from low-rent growth. As such, I still believe a Buy rating is warranted for BSR.

Risks

My calculations show that after the latest portfolio transactions, leverage as a percentage of enterprise value has increased to 61%. This is quite high relative to peers, especially when we factor in the uncertain outlook for revenue growth.

As such, BSR is now increasingly dependent on the Fed lowering interest rates, which will allow the company to refinance debt at favorable rates. Currently all debt is fixed rate (accounting for the use of hedges), with only 13% maturing over the next year:

The bad news is that even with this laddered maturity structure, the 3.8% average cost of debt is likely to creep up, perhaps reaching 4.5% once the Fed is at its 3% neutral rate. As such, the outlook for AFFO growth is quite low in light of refinancing headwinds.

Last but not least, the period of below-inflation rent growth may continue longer than expected. This again will pressure BSR's AFFO growth outlook, somewhat justifying the high market-implied cap rate.

Conclusion

BSR Real Estate Investment Trust has slumped by circa 11% over the past month, erasing positive momentum relative to the VNQ ETF earlier in the year:

While the company's ongoing portfolio transactions continue to cloud its financial outlook, my calculations show that BSR should deliver AFFO of about $0.19/share in Q3 2025, in line with the one in Q2 2025. This puts the current AFFO run-rate multiple at about 15.5x, quite attractive for a residential REIT.

While the AFFO growth outlook is muted thanks to refinancing headwinds and weak rent growth, the company's enterprise-level valuation remains attractive, with BSR trading at a market-implied cap rate of 7.4%.

As such, I continue to believe that a Buy rating is warranted for the company, notwithstanding risks primarily stemming from elevated leverage.

Thank you for reading.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Market Premium For 10-Year Treasury Yield Falls To 12-Month Low
data.description:
The spread for the US 10-year Treasury yield over a “fair value” estimate has narrowed to the smallest gap in a year, based on the average estimate for three models run by CapitalSpectator.com.

The latest analysis, which is monthly and reflects numbers through September, reflects an ongoing decline in the market premium this year. The current fair value estimate for the 10-year yield is 3.85%, which is 27 basis points below the market’s average 4.12% rate in September.

The latest downturn in the market premium over the fair value estimate extends a downside trend that’s been unfolding in recent history. The downshift highlights a mean-reversion tendency in the 10-year yield’s shifting premium and discounts relative to the fair value estimate. For the currency cycle, the premium peaked at nearly 1.4 percentage points in late 2023.

Keep in mind that the latest estimate reflects modeling that’s missing several economic data points due to the government shutdown. For example, the September update on consumer inflation, which was scheduled for release earlier this week, has been delayed. The missing numbers to date have had a minimal effect on average fair value analytics due to the use of three models that use a wide range of economic and market data. Presumably the government will reopen soon, and the delayed reports will be issued. Shortly after, we’ll re-run the analysis and present an updated fair value report.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Autoliv: Upside Is Likely, I'm Buying Into Q3 '25
data.description:
It's been a while since I last covered Autoliv, Inc. (NYSE:ALV, Nasdaq Stockholm: ALIV). This company has one of the better market positions in the manufacturing and research of safety technology in the automotive sector. At the right price, and given that you have enough of a buffer to make up for the complex volatility and changes in the automotive sector.

As long as you have this, you can take advantage of the company's excellent fundamentals, including the amount of debt, its market position, and its quite good yield (close to 3% even now).

I was actually wrong on Autoliv since my last article on Seeking Alpha. This company doesn't get a lot of coverage love here on SA, but I believe it's worth coverage after its latest results and with the latest estimates for 2026-2028.

So what we'll do in this article is look at the latest results, what caused the last slight drop, and what upside we have for the near term and short term. I will also qualify my thesis for the company, both using short-term estimates and longer-term estimates to cater to both types of investors a little more. Given that the company's Q3 '25 has come today, you can see this as an earnings review, with me making a case that I believe it's a good investment. I, however, bought yesterday prior to market opening.

Let's see what we have here.

Autoliv - Recent trends are complex, but the overall picture is one of a positive nature

Q3 '25 was a superb quarter for the company. I justify saying this because of record-level sales, with excellent operating income increase, margin expansions, and earnings per share increase. Top-line growth was also ensured.

What's more impressive is that this expansion and growth came despite significant regional pressure in the LVP market. The LVP market has, as you can see in my articles on general automotive companies, been negative for some time. This has also been impacted by tariff considerations and macro.

But the company's performance means that the typical correlation between the LVP and Autoliv's performance, which has typically been high, has been broken. The company is doing significantly better than the overall LVP market.

This picture also includes cost improvements, gross margin improvements, lower SG&A and research costs, and while OCF is down quarter-to-quarter, on a longer-term trend, it's one of improvement over the past 5-10 year period.

As we can see, the drivers of recent outperformance come from both European brands, Asian brands (ex-China), and >Chinese brands as well, to some extent. This includes model launches like the new Subaru Outback, the Kia PV5, the new Jeep Compass, and others.

The biggest positive that I would focus on here is operating income. A significant improvement in margin when you have sub-10% margins is very significant, with both RoE and ROCE showing improvements as well. The company made a dividend increase, but repurchased a much lower amount of shares than previously, once again something I approve of, given the now-improved valuation.

The company's margin improvements originated in the company's plans for efficiency. Specifically, operational efficiency, including higher accuracy, is next to organic sales growth. The company has been on a 10-year trend/plan to improve its working capital as well, lowering it from around 17-18% of sales back in -19, to a level of close to 12-13% now. When you're looking at a company like Autoliv, that's very heavy in inventory and manufacturing, such an improvement is not to be underestimated.

Neither is the fact that the company managed this without increasing its leverage by any noteworthy amount. As of the latest quarterly results, that leverage is still at 1.3x or below the 1.5x mark.

What about tariffs? What happens in the U.S. ratchets this up?

Autoliv's impacts on tariffs are expected a $100M per year. This is mostly for inputs from metal, such as steel and aluminum, but also parts that are not USMCA compliant - and while the company is working to minimize these impacts, I do believe that some tariff impacts will be seen. I estimate $50-$70M.

Going forward, the company remains a play one, on its own efficiency, but still two, mostly on LVP. The current estimate is for a 1.3% growth, in line with GDP, given new drivelines and continued EV developments. However, given that the company has identified several new drivers of improvements outside of this, there is an expectation of profit margin improvement and earnings increases here outside of. The company, in fact, officially expects such a margin expansion.

Based on this, I say two things. First, there is enough to work with to estimate a higher price target, or PT, and fair value for the company with these improvements, because these were higher estimates than I originally worked with.

Secondly, I believe the company is very attractive here after Q3 '25 being published, and it's a good idea to buy the company as long as it's under my PT.

Autoliv valuation says we can enter

At the time of writing this article, this company's native ticker ALV is trading at a P/E of ~13x on average. This is low for this company, which tends towards a valuation of 16.5x for the 5-year average, and 15.3x P/E on the long-term 20-year average. So, on the basis of this, the company is undervalued. The upside to the longer-term valuation at this time is higher than 15% - significantly higher.

So even on a conservative basis, this is more than 20% annualized. In terms of a fair value, I give the company 15x for 2027E, which comes to $190/share. I justify this high valuation and the growth that this entails, based on the company's tendency to not only hit but to beat valuations 50% of the time on a 1-year basis with a 10% margin of error.

The $190/share valuation target implies that to get a 15% annualized upside, we need to buy at below $140/share, which comes to my new, updated PT for the company. This means that we're currently in Buy territory (still, since my last article). Even in the scenario of significant earnings pressure and lower valuation, it would take a lot for your returns to go negative here, given the valuation. The last time we saw this attractive valuation was the period of 2024, when I bought my shares. Let this be a lesson that when this company drops below double digits in price, you Buy it.

Alternatives to the company do, of course, exist. Competition comes in the form of companies BorgWarner Inc. (BWA), Magna International Inc. (MGA), and Takata, primarily. I've said it before when reviewing Magna and Lear Corporation (LEA), that the entire automotive industry, including suppliers, is currently trading on the cheap. Out of these mentioned peers, I believe Lear and Magna could make for interesting investments next to Autoliv. Lear is BBB rated, not as good fundamentally as Autoliv (debt), but with an upside that I find very hard to deny. Even on the very lowest long-term P/E forecasts, this company has a higher potential return than does Autoliv. That's worth considering as well.

I recently exited my positions in banking. I have partially exited the salmon industry (Salmar) after a short-term return of 30%, which comes to over 100% annualized after having bought the native ticker of ADR SALRY on the cheap back in June of this year. This highlights how I both work short-term and long-term, going mostly by valuation these days.

The reason I mention this is that some of this capital, today, will be used to increase my position in Autoliv. I have increased my stake to about 1.3% in total of my total portfolio, and depending on development, there is more upside to be had here in the long term (and potentially more investment opportunities too).

As of today (that's the 16th of October), I will also be investing 10% of the sales proceeds into Lear as well.

Risks and other relevant considerations for Autoliv as an investment are currently as follows.

Risks & Considerations

The reason for being more negative on the company is related to growth potential, specifically the LVP market. I believe that automotive/LVP growth will be slower going forward than it has been historically. Slower growth means that even in the face of increased buybacks and internal efficiencies, it could be argued to keep this company at an overall lower share price target. That's not even mentioning the competitive nature of the segment because I believe this to be less relevant than other analysts seem to, given Autoliv's technology leadership.

I will argue that I keep the company's PT at a relatively low level, given that I use conservative long-term averages rather than short-term to establish my overall targets - but it's fair, if you will, to put it even lower. Please note, as seen above, however, that even if you were to put the company a lot lower in price, the upside would still be 15% annualized or above here.

My stance is that the company offers a more than 15% annualized upside based on what I view as a conservative NTM P/E of 14-16x for 2025-2026, and 15-16x P/E for 2027 on current estimates. The company's yield is not fantastic, but the combination of yield, safety, and upside is good enough to justify it still.

My thesis is as follows.

Thesis

Autoliv is one of the more solid automotive safety companies in the world, but unfortunately still has massive ties to light automotive vehicle production. The company's upside as of Q3 '25 is still intact, and i believe in fact better than in a long time. Because of the improvements in Q3 '25, I further believe that when the company reports (given estimates for H2 '25, as well as the overall industry valuation), the company will rise even more going forward.

The aforementioned upside is still volatile, but the company has some excellent growth potential from a mix of at least 1-2% annual LVP trends, better synergies, operational improvements, and other bullish potentials.

I now have a longer-term stake in Autoliv, with a projected timeframe of at the very least 2027. I view the primary risks as being tariffs and LVP overall, but I believe the company is positioned well enough to manage both of them quite nicely.

For the various theses for short and long-term, I say the following.

My short-term investment case (6-12 months):

The narrative for the short term isn't that different from the long term. The company is still attractive here. I believe we can see a swing in the right direction even from a good H2 '25, and I believe the company's upside will outweigh tariff threats. This means that the company in the short term is a Buy here.

My medium-to-long-term investment case (up to and beyond 5 years) is as follows:

For the longer term, I would continue to argue that there are few downsides to the company until 2027E as an investment, which is also the reason why I am positive about the stock here. I consider the company a Buy for the long term as well. I don't see any particular risk that makes this something to avoid at this time.

Here are my criteria and how the company fulfills them (bolded).

  • This company is overall qualitative.
  • This company is fundamentally safe/conservative & well-run.
  • This company pays a well-covered dividend.
  • This company is currently cheap.
  • This company has a realistic upside that is high enough, based on earnings growth or multiple expansion/reversion.

The company fulfills 3 out of 3 quality indicators and 1 out of 2 valuation indicators. That means 4/5 "stars" here, which is enough for an attractive thesis for the company. Based on this, and a valuation target of $140/share where I see a risk/reward-adjusted upside that's attractive enough for me. I give the company a Buy rating as of October 2025.

data.title: 2025 Chinese Jewellery Retailer Insights: Gold And Self-Wear Remain Key
data.description:
Jewellery market trends through the lens of retailers

Consumers have adopted a cautious spending attitude towards gold jewellery amid the gold price rally and Chinese jewellery retailers have continued to face fierce competition in 2025. Price competition and product homogenisation have translated into industry consolidation: major Chinese jewellery brands have started to reduce points of sale (POS).

But there are bright spots and further potential in this seemingly saturated market as consumer spending on gold jewellery has remained elevated. Gold jewellery products are still attractive to consumers.

To explore current trends and future opportunities, we conducted our 2025 annual Chinese jewellery retailer research in partnership with China Gold News for the sixth consecutive year. Complementing our recently published 2025 Chinese Gold Jewellery Consumer Insights, we dived into the market through the lens of the retailer and found that:

  • Gold products continue to dominate Chinese jewellery retailer sales, with Hard Pure gold products outperforming others
  • Heritage and Hard Pure gold jewellery products remain key sources of retailers’ profit
  • Although gold jewellery products are becoming lighter in weight, consumer spending rose
  • Buying for self-indulgent wear remains key in retailer jewellery sales
  • Retailer optimism around gold product sales is intact; self-indulgent buying may continue to drive business in the future.

Chinese gold jewellery demand in H1: spending elevated, volumes down

Chinese gold jewellery demand volumes experienced continued weaknesses during the first half of 2025 (Chart 1). As our Gold Demand Trends report noted, the weakest Q2 since 2007 took China’s total H1 gold jewellery demand to 194t, 28% lower y/y, the lowest H1 since 2009. Yet while volumes were down, consumer spending on gold jewellery during the first half reached RMB137bn (US$19bn), on a par with 2024 and not too far from the record set in H1 2013 (RMB163bn).

Chart 1: Volumes down, spending elevated

Chinese gold jewellery demand in H1 in quantity and value*

Chinese consumers have not lost interest in gold jewellery. And as mentioned in our recently published 2025 Chinese gold jewellery consumer insights, the ownership of gold jewellery among Chinese consumers reached 81%, the highest of all fashion and lifestyle items. More significantly, the majority of those who have purchased gold jewellery before would consider buying again in the near future (75%).

Looking forward to H2, seasonality will likely help improve tonnage demand for gold jewellery. Although total demand volumes in full year 2025 may decline compared to 2024, we expect consumer gold jewellery expenditure to remain near levels seen in recent years.

That said, uncertainties prevail for jewellers. For instance, should they reduce the share of gold products in their stores – gold inventories have been built up over recent years – and increase other categories? Has the dominance of gold products decreased in 2025? Which gold products contribute the highest profit? And how have combinations of 24K gold and various other materials performed? By reviewing past performance and examining the future through the lens of the retailer, we believe we can provide guidance for market participants, particularly at a time when the industry is consolidating.

The 2025 Chinese jewellery retailer insights

2025 marks our sixth annual Chinese jewellery retailer survey in collaboration with China Gold News. We collected 576 effective responses via a combination of online questionnaires and offline in-depth interviews from Chinese jewellery retail shop managers and regional heads. We captured market insights from various tiers of cities, covering areas with strong economic growth and regions with relatively lower incomes (Chart 2). Our survey includes both jewellery shops at malls (56%) and street stores (44%), representing different dynamics.

Chart 2: Our survey scope in 2025

Gold dominates but jewellery’s share shrinks modestly

During the first half, gold products remain the most dominant force in China, but divergence between jewellery and investment emerges (Chart 3).

Compared to H1 2024, we saw a modest decline in gold jewellery’s dominance as the surging gold price depressed sales to some extent. But the attractive gold price performance played well to gold investment demand, as seen by the rise in bars and coins' contribution to retailer sales – trends we detail in our Q2 Gold Demand Trends.

We observed continued shrinkage in sales contributions from diamond and platinum jewellery products. But jewellery items such as gems, jade and pearls were the bright spot – these colourful and more affordable accessories captured rising attention from young consumers.

Chart 3: Though declined, gold kept the largest market share in China’s jewellery sector

Contribution of various categories to Chinese jewellery retailer revenue so far in 2025 (in value)*

Hard Pure gold product sales robust and “Gold+” rising in importance

Among various gold products, 32% of retailers saw growth in sales of Hard Pure gold jewellery, the best-performing category in H1 (Chart 4). Due to their lighter weight, which translates to better affordability, innovative design and smart combination with other materials, Hard Pure gold products outperformed other categories.

Chart 4: Hard Pure gold jewellery saw robust performance

% of retailers that experienced increased sales during H1*

Bar and coin sales saw similar strength. Our data shows that the 293t H1 2025 total is the strongest since 2013, 26% higher y/y. This is mainly driven by factors such as gold’s robust performance and Chinese households’ elevated tendency towards savings.1

Compared to 2024, more retailers have seen growth in Heritage gold jewellery products this year. Due to the surging gold price, most retailers have promoted lighter-weight versions of these products in recent years; with their better affordability and still-rich Chinese cultural value, they continue to attract investors of all ages.

Our survey dug deeper this year, exploring the contribution of products that combine 24K gold with other materials (Chart 5). Products made of 24K gold braided into wristbands have become an essential source of revenue to many retailers. These are attractive to young consumers as they can choose combinations of different braided ropes and gold items, which are typically small, light, and therefore more affordable to youngsters.

The combination of gold – typically lighter-weight Hard Pure products or other types with similar craft – and enamel is also key to many retailers. We highlight in our Gold Demand Trends how the enamel touch is attractive to consumers, making gold jewellery products colourful yet affordable.

Chart 5: The innovative combination of gold and other materials is gaining importance

% of retailers who agree that the following items are essential to their sales revenue in H1

Retailers recognise that these combinations add value to gold products, helping to boost their profit margins and attracting young consumers; consequently they are increasing attention and effort towards their promotion. We believe China’s gold jewellery products are transitioning from “plain gold” to “gold+other materials”. And it is likely that this trend is here to stay given the surging gold price and the need for young consumers to express their personality through their jewellery.

As to profitability, retailers mentioned that Heritage gold products and Hard Pure gold items contributed the most to their profits in H1 2025 (Chart 6).The Chinese cultural element and wide design choices added value to these two categories, enabling higher retailer margins while being highly attractive to consumers.

We also noted that, in general, the profitability in most gold product categories has lowered compared to 2023. This is a result of industry involution, a phenomenon where retailers compete by lowering their margins at a time when the sector’s tonnage demand weakened significantly. And although sales of bars and coins grew notably, their profit contribution remained mute – as consumers typically favour low-cost products for investment purposes.

Chart 6: Heritage and Hard Pure gold products remain key profit contributors for retailers

Ranking of profitability score*

Lighter weight products prevail but unit total spending rises

Our survey results indicate that the contribution of lighter-weight gold products to retailer sales has risen. Retailers told us that gold products under 10 grams accounted for 45% of their gold jewellery sales in H1 2025 (Chart 7). This represents a notable increase from 37% in 2023, when we last asked this question. This is perhaps not surprising, given the rising popularity of lighter weight gold products amid the gold price surge and the industry’s efforts in design innovation. The rocketing gold price was also responsible for reduced sales of chunky items above 20 grams. But sales of products weighted between 10 and 20 grams remained stable.

Chart 7: The surging price suppressed the weight of gold products sold

Product shares of total gold sales across different weights (inner: 2023; outer: 2025)

But it is a different story when we examine the share of gold products by different unit prices (Chart 8). We found that consumer spending on gold jewellery in 2025 is generally rising compared to 2023: a product bought at the same weight in 2025 costs much more than it did two years previously.

This is consistent with our observed demand trends: gold jewellery demand, in value terms, remained 35% above its 10-year average in H1 2025. In value terms, the market is expanding rather than shrinking.

Chart 8: Consumer spending on gold jewellery rose

Product shares of total gold sales across different unit price

Self-wear remains the most vital driver of consumer jewellery purchases

Our survey data shows that purchases for “self-wear” remain the largest contributor to jewellery retailer revenues. Accounting for 37% of retailer jewellery sales – much greater than 2024’s 27% – self-indulgent buying continued to drive consumer purchase decisions. This is confirmed by our 2025 Chinese Gold Jewellery Consumer Insights, which revealed that the majority of consumers purchase fine gold jewellery for themselves.

Meanwhile, the share of wedding-related jewellery sales shrunk to 19%, compared to 2024’s 24%. As we have previously noted, the long-term trend of declining marriages is weighing on wedding-related gold jewellery purchases. And the fact that many couples now purchase their wedding accessories for daily wear too has blurred the line between “wedding” and “self-wear” buying.

It is also notable that while the importance of other key jewellery purchase moments has declined, gifting between couples remains stable. A widening product range and rising promotional efforts from retailers at key occasions, such as Chinese Valentine’s Day, have helped to drive gifting choices for many young couples.

Chart 9: Self-indulgent buying remains the largest sales contributor for jewellery retailers

Contribution to retailer revenues during H1 2025 by various jewellery purchase moments

Middle-aged consumers buy the most but younger groups are vital

Middle-aged consumers remain the most important revenue source for jewellery retailers (Chart 10). Compared to 2024, we found that young consumers’ contribution to sales reduced slightly while older customers purchased more. This is likely due to affordability issues as the gold price soared – the young generation is usually less wealthy than their older counterparts. This is consistent with our 2025 Chinese gold jewellery consumer insights, in which we found that gold jewellery buyers are mostly middle-aged and married with higher incomes.

Nonetheless, youngsters continue to contribute more than a third to jewellery retailer sales in 2025. Our consumer insights indicated that gold jewellery ownership among young consumers has risen notably, suggesting that the emotional link between gold jewellery and young individuals has strengthened over recent years.

We believe that young consumers will remain a vital force going forward, underpinned by a rising recognition of their Chinese cultural identity, which is rich in gold jewellery products, especially Heritage gold items. Also, as the younger generation becomes more financially savvy, the investment potential of gold jewellery will likely further capture their attention. The industry’s constant innovation towards making gold jewellery products lighter, more trendy and more colourful – by combining gold with other materials – will likely continue to attract this younger demographic.

Chart 10: Young consumers remain key despite a modest decline in sales contribution

Jewellery retailer sales sources by age (inner: 2024; outer: 2025)

Outlook

Gold optimism intact

Retailers remain confident about gold product sales in the next one to two years (Chart 11). Our data shows that 47% of retailers anticipate growth in gold sales: 14% of our total sample believe such growth could be sharp – the highest of all jewellery categories – and 33% expect a more modest increase. Such optimism is roughly in line with our results from 2024, although the surging gold price in recent years has made jewellers adopt a more cautious approach than they had in 2023.

Chart 11: Optimism around gold remains intact

Q: What is your expectation of the following categories in the next 1~2 years?

Self-wear is seen as the engine that will drive future jewellery sales

Nearly half of retailers believe that self-indulgent purchases will remain their most important sales contributor. In contrast, over 50% of our sample anticipates that continued contraction in wedding-related sales is likely for the reasons mentioned above.

Chart 12: Most retailers remain confident about self-indulgent sales

Expectations of jewellery retailers for each purchasing moment

Caution ahead

Retailers remain cautious in their gold inventory strategies. Facing uncertainty around the country’s economic growth and rising funding costs amid the surging gold price, the share of retailers planning to reduce their gold inventory surpassed that preparing to increase for the second year. In 2025, 42% of our sample tell us that they plan to reduce their gold inventory – although the majority say that reduction will be a modest one.

Chart 13: Retailers plan to continue to dial down their gold inventories in 2025

Q: How are you planning to alter your gold inventory?

This is perhaps not surprising, as ongoing consolidation within the industry has led to fewer POS. And lighter inventory pressure could help ease retailers’ funding cost pressure against the current backdrop. As gold product margins are squeezed and sales become more difficult amid the surging price, retailers are looking to other sources of profitability.

Meanwhile, over 60% of retailers plan to increase the number of lighter weight gold products in their stores (items under 10 grams), up from 56% in 2024. And 55% of our sample say they will increase their share of gold products between RMB2,000 and RMB5,000, higher than all other price ranges.

Conclusion

The soaring gold price and consumer caution have dented gold jewellery demand volumes in China over recent years. But total spending on gold jewellery has remained elevated. These products still attract consumers and there may be further opportunities for retailers to unlock.

From our 2025 Chinese gold jewellery retailer survey, we learned that among all jewellery items, gold products continue to dominate retailer sales. Of these, sales of Hard Pure gold jewellery, bars and coins, and Heritage items outperformed others. Hard Pure and Heritage gold products remain the most vital profit source for retailers – investment products rank the lowest in margin contribution despite their popularity. Meanwhile, 24K gold braided into wrist ropes and combined with enamel has become key to retailer sales.

In general, we noted that Hard Pure gold technology has been widely used on many other types of gold products. And the trend of lighter weight and more intricate design continues to soar in popularity – making gold jewellery more affordable whilst offering higher margins to retailers.

Although gold product sales are declining, consumers are willing to pay higher prices. Self-wear remains their key jewellery purchase moment, followed by wedding demand. And more than a third of these consumers are between 18 and 24 years old.

Looking forward, retailers remain confident about future gold product sales, particularly those driven by self-indulgent purchases. Nonetheless, their optimism has cooled compared to previous years and they remain cautious as they adjust their gold inventories.

Footnotes:

1See: Urban Household Savings, July 2025.

Original Post
